Stunt Difficulty Rubric
Click any skill cell to see scoring notes and video examples. Starting value is determined by a minimum of two different skills successfully executed within a range (one may be a toss) by the majority of the maximum number of stunt groups.
📌
How to use this rubric: Click any cell to open a popup with skill notes, judging tips, and video examples. Use the range legend below to filter by difficulty level.
Beginner (0.0 – 1.0)
Intermediate (1.25 – 3.25)
Advanced (3.5 – 5.5)
Elite (5.75 – 7.75)
Super Elite (8.0 – 10.0)

Pyramid Difficulty Rubric
Click any skill cell to see scoring notes and video examples. Starting value is determined by a minimum of three different skills successfully executed within a range by the majority of the maximum number of stunt groups.
📌
How to use this rubric: Click any cell to open a popup with skill notes, judging tips, and video examples. Use the range legend below to filter by difficulty level.
💡
The number in parentheses (1 or 2) refers to the number of pyramid bracers utilized during the transition. Legal unbraced skills or those utilizing fewer bracers should receive more credit within the range.
Beginner (0.0 – 1.0)
Intermediate (1.25 – 3.25)
Advanced (3.5 – 5.5)
Elite (5.75 – 7.75)
Super Elite (8.0 – 10.0)

Skill Locator
A working guide for coaches & officials: answer a few questions about a skill and find exactly where it scores.
⚠️ Note: This locator is not for use with Single Base Stunts or Tosses — refer to those sections of the rubric directly.
Step 1

Identify Your Skill

Answer each question based on the elements of your skill. The tool weighs each selection together and highlights the matching skill in the rubric.

1Does the skill invert?
No
Yes
Flyer's shoulders drop below the waist at any point.
↓
2Does the skill release?
No
Yes
Flyer leaves all bases' & spotters' hands, at any point.
↓
•Number of flyer's feet in bases' hands right before the release:

One foot
Neither foot
Both feet

One Foot: A foot-to-foot swap (ex: Low to High, Switch Up, Tic Toc, High to High)
Neither Foot: Both feet are free from contact with bases prior to the release (ex: BHS Up, Hand in Hand, Horizontal Release)
Both Feet: Flyer has both feet in the hands of bases prior to release (ex: Ball Up)
↓
3Twist amount
No twist
¼
½
¾
Full+
↓
4Starting level
Below Prep
Prep
Extended / IBP / ABP
↓
5Ending level
Below Prep
Prep
Extended / IBP / ABP
1Number of bracers?
0
1
2
Number of bracer flyers in contact with the skill flyer. Select '0' for Unbraced skills.
↓
2Inversion?
No
Yes
Braced Roll
Braced Flip
No: Flyer does not invert during skill
Yes: Flyer is inverted but without hip over head rotation (ex: Inverted Low to High, Hand in Hand)
Braced Roll: Flyer is inverted with hip over head rotation while maintaining contact with a base/spotter throughout.
Braced Flip: Flyer is inverted with hip over head rotation while being released from bases/spotter.
↓
3Release?
No
Yes
Flyer leaves all bases' & spotters' hands, at any point.
↓

How the Logic Works

Rules & Definitions

The reasoning behind the automatic matching above, for reference.

Click below to launch the interactive training guide covering how single base skills are scored inside a pyramid — a common source of scoring confusion, walked through step by step.

🧩
Single Base Pyramid Skills
Click to Launch Training Guide
Single Base Pyramid Skills — Training Guide
Built from the 2026–2027 VHSL Competitive Cheer Rubric (version updated June 8, 2026)
For reference & training only — always confirm scores against the official printed rubric and judges' rulings.
Key Notes & Additional Info
Important additional information that applies across both the Stunt and Pyramid rubrics. These rules affect how difficulty is scored and where skills land within a range.

Front Spots

Front spots (stunt groups of more than 4) or less than full participation from the maximum number of possible stunt groups will decrease the value of a skill within the range.

Body Positions

IBP: Liberty, Hitch, Torch
ABP: Heel Stretch, Arabesque, Scorpion, Bow & Arrow, etc.

Body positions alone do not receive credit for difficulty — they must be connected to a skill/transition.

Momentum Through IBP

Momentum from a skill/transition directly into an ABP may pass through lib/platform/dangle, but must be continuous without pause in order to be credited together.

Dismounts & Transitions

If a transition or dismount meets the requirement of a rubric skill, it should be given credit as such. Don't overlook transitions — they count!

Inversions

Flyer's shoulders are below their waist, regardless of foot placement. Inverted transitions must remain inverted at the initiation of the bases' movement to be credited.

Single Base Skills

Must begin from the ground/load, end, and maintain throughout the skill a single base (plus spotter where required). Skills that start as full group and release to single base do not qualify.

Pyramid Bracers (1 or 2)

The number in parentheses indicates how many bracers are used. Unbraced skills or those using fewer bracers receive more credit within the same range.

Unbraced Pyramid Timing

Unbraced pyramid transitions must connect at the top of the skill within 2 counts to be considered a pyramid and receive pyramid credit.

Ripples

Ripples are allowed within the stunt/pyramid sequence but should be clearly defined. It must be very obvious that athletes are not being recycled.

Pyramid Variety

In pyramid, a variety of flyers involved in transitional skills may increase score within the range and/or be given consideration in overall impression.

Full Up/Around Variations

For all levels, the variation or modified grip version of the skill will be scored lower in the range than the true/traditional version.

Twisting — Flyer Rotation

With the exception of ¼ twisting releases, the majority of the rotation must be completed by the flyer. Bases walking a stunt around does not constitute a full up.

📧 Have a video example to contribute or a skill you'd like clarification on? Submit it to the VHSL Cheer Officials — we'll review and add them to this module.   TheNVCOA@gmail.com
Majority Rules
Understanding majority is essential for every scoring decision. Use the interactive calculator below to quickly find majority thresholds for any team size, then review the key majority rules.

Majority Calculator

Drag the slider to set squad size — majority thresholds update instantly.

Athletes on Mat
15
Athletes
3 5 8 10 12 15 18 20
▼ Full Reference Table
Athletes Stunt Groups
(Single Base or Full Group)		 Pyramid Skill Groups
(Non-Bracer Groups)		 Jumps/Tumbling
203 groups2 skill groups11 athletes
193 groups2 skill groups10 athletes
183 groups2 skill groups10 athletes
173 groups2 skill groups9 athletes
163 groups2 skill groups9 athletes
152 groups1 skill group8 athletes
142 groups1 skill group8 athletes
132 groups1 skill group7 athletes
122 groups1 skill group7 athletes
112 groups1 skill group6 athletes
102 groups1 skill group6 athletes
9 and under2 groups1 skill group5 athletes
Key Majority Rules
Ripples Are Allowed
Ripples (sequential timing of the same exact skill) are allowed within stunt, pyramid, and tumbling sequences but should be clearly defined. It should be very obvious that athletes are not being recycled.
Front Spots & Non-Participation
Front spots (stunt groups of more than 4) or less than full participation from the maximum number of possible stunt groups will decrease the value of a skill within the range.
Pyramid Majority (16+ Athletes)
Teams of 16 or more are required to have at least TWO different flyers (in addition to any bracers) completing the required rubric skills. These may be a connected 2x2 pyramid, two separate mini pyramids, or any combination. Ripples are allowed but should be obvious and clearly defined.
If Majority Fails
If a required skill falls before completion, judges look for other transition/lower-level skills. If skills only occur in different ranges without meeting minimums for one range, the score drops to the lowest range executed. If no majority at all → score drops to the 0–1 range.

Difficulty Calculator
💡
HOW TO USEEnter the number of athletes, then click on each skill. In the list below each rubric, enter the number of groups performing that skill, then click Calculate to determine the overall difficulty range.

Click on the rubric image to select skills.

Enter the number of stunt groups for each skill:
